description: Curating the token budget: compaction, just-in-time retrieval, agentic memory.

questions.question: What is 'context engineering'?
options: Designing longer and increasingly detailed instruction templates for single-turn interactions
The set of strategies for curating and maintaining the optimal set of tokens in a model's context during inference
Engineering high-performance distributed caching servers and relational databases to store long conversation histories
Translating natural language user inputs into the specific source languages that the model encountered during pre-training
questions.explanation: As defined in Anthropic's engineering guidance, context engineering manages everything that enters the model's limited context — system instructions, tools, retrieved data, message history — treating context as a finite resource to be curated, not just a prompt to be written.

questions.question: How does context engineering differ from prompt engineering?
options: They represent identical optimization concepts, with context engineering simply serving as an enterprise marketing rebrand of traditional instructions used for chatbot applications
Prompt engineering applies exclusively to generative image, video, and audio models, while context engineering is restricted strictly to text-only user chat interfaces
Prompt engineering focuses on writing effective instructions; context engineering manages the entire evolving context (tools, history, retrieved data, memory) across an agent's many inference turns
Context engineering completely replaces the need for writing prompt instructions by dynamically constructing and injecting variable context parameters on the backend server side
questions.explanation: Prompt engineering optimizes the words of an instruction, which suits one-shot tasks. Agents loop over many turns and generate ever more data, so the harder question becomes which subset of all available information should occupy the context at each step — that curation is context engineering.

questions.question: What phenomenon does the term 'context rot' describe?
options: The gradual degradation of pre-trained model weights stored on low-quality storage disks over extended periods of inactivity
The risk of training data representations becoming obsolete or factually inaccurate after the model's public knowledge cutoff date
The automated pruning and deletion of old customer conversation log files from cloud servers to meet compliance standards
The decline in a model's ability to recall and utilize information effectively as the active context window size increases
questions.explanation: Context rot — named in a 2025 Chroma research report and echoed in Anthropic's context-engineering guidance — is the observed decline in retrieval and reasoning quality as input length grows, even well within the advertised context window. It's why more context is not automatically better.

questions.question: What is meant by a model's 'attention budget'?
options: The financial budget allocated to compute resources for attention layers during the initial pre-training run of the model
The finite capacity a model has for attending across its context — which every added token depletes
The absolute limit of concurrent user requests and sessions that a single model instance can actively process in memory
A subscription quota system designed to limit how many prompt questions a user is permitted to submit within one hour
questions.explanation: Because transformer attention relates every token to every other token, effective attention gets stretched thinner as context grows. Treating attention as a budget leads to the guiding heuristic: find the smallest set of high-signal tokens that maximizes the likelihood of the desired outcome.

questions.question: In long-running agents, what is 'compaction'?
options: Compressing the neural network's weights into a lower precision quantization format to speed up initialization
Zipping and archiving historical database conversation logs before sending them to long-term cold file storage
Summarizing the conversation so far and restarting the context with that summary, freeing space while preserving key details
Programmatically stripping out redundant whitespaces and comment blocks from raw prompts to minimize token usage
questions.explanation: When a conversation approaches the context limit, the agent distills it — decisions made, unresolved issues, key facts — and continues with the distilled version. Claude Code's auto-compact does exactly this as the window fills.

questions.question: What is 'just-in-time' context retrieval for agents?
options: Pre-loading and indexing every potentially relevant document into memory before the agent receives its first query
The agent loads data at runtime via tools (file paths, queries, links) exactly when needed, instead of ingesting everything up front
Streaming output text tokens back to the client interface immediately as they are generated by the model during inference
Caching and reusing the previous day's entire conversation context window to reduce startup compute latency for new runs
questions.explanation: Rather than stuffing all data into context up front, the agent keeps lightweight references (paths, queries, IDs) and fetches content on demand — mirroring how humans use file systems and bookmarks instead of memorizing everything.

questions.question: What is 'structured note-taking' (agentic memory)?
options: The agent regularly writes key state to persistent storage outside the context window (e.g., a NOTES.md or to-do file) and reads it back later
Applying custom style instructions that force the language model to format all its internal reasoning states and final responses as structured markdown tables
Executing automated fine-tuning runs on the language model using the user's personal notes database on a weekly offline schedule
Applying cryptographic algorithms to encrypt the raw conversation history database before storing it on cloud server instances
questions.explanation: By persisting notes outside the window, an agent maintains goals, progress, and learned details across compactions and long task horizons — memory that survives even when the context is reset.

questions.question: How do sub-agent architectures help manage context?
options: Sub-agents are forced to share a single global context window, allowing them to access the exact same history to minimize concurrent GPU memory allocation
Sub-agents execute their tasks using smaller, highly compressed model variants to optimize response latency and token costs
Sub-agents completely bypass the need for external data retrieval by memorizing the vector database during training phases
Each sub-agent handles its assigned task in a separate, isolated context window and returns only a high-level summary to the main orchestrator
questions.explanation: A sub-agent might burn tens of thousands of tokens searching and reading, but hand back only a distilled summary. The orchestrator gets the insight without inheriting the exploration's token cost — isolation that keeps the main context focused.
